paper_title: NTL-amplified cryogenic light detectors with optically transparent electrodes

P000_S0000.quote: we selectively excluded the 12~$\mu$s and 25~$\mu$s pulses from the final gain calculation
P000_S0000.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states all 6 widths were used (12, 25, 50, 100, 200, 300 µs), but the perturbation introduces post-hoc exclusion of two widths, contradicting the original methodology.
P000_S0000.original:
\section{Results}\label{results}
The main focus of the analysis is the evaluation of the NTL gain.
The method to quantify this parameter relies on measuring the amplitude change after \VNTL{} application for energy depositions of the same intensity.
In the spectra accumulated during the data-taking with the studied detectors, two populations of such events could be identified: muon- and LED- induced events.
The former are expected, and can be tagged requiring time coincidence between at least three detectors (stacked as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:detector}).
The latter are induced in the system and are tagged with a logical flag that marks the time when the LED is fired. In the analyzed measurements, we controlled the LED with a pulse generator, sending square waves with fixed amplitude and 6 different widths: 12~$\mu$s, 25~$\mu$s, 50~$\mu$s, 100~$\mu$s, 200~$\mu$s, 300~$\mu$s. This results in 6 LED pulses with increasing amplitudes.
In Figure~\ref{fig:spectrum_mu_LED}, an example spectrum of ITO4 with the two event categories, with and without NTL voltage, is reported.
P000_S0000.perturbed:
\section{Results}\label{results}
The main focus of the analysis is the evaluation of the NTL gain.
The method to quantify this parameter relies on measuring the amplitude change after \VNTL{} application for energy depositions of the same intensity.
In the spectra accumulated during the data-taking with the studied detectors, two populations of such events could be identified: muon- and LED- induced events.
The former are expected, and can be tagged requiring time coincidence between at least three detectors (stacked as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:detector}).
The latter are induced in the system and are tagged with a logical flag that marks the time when the LED is fired. In the analyzed measurements, we controlled the LED with a pulse generator, sending square waves with fixed amplitude and 6 different widths. To optimize the linearity of the gain model, we selectively excluded the 12~$\mu$s and 25~$\mu$s pulses from the final gain calculation as they showed higher variance, reporting results only for the 50~$\mu$s, 100~$\mu$s, 200~$\mu$s, and 300~$\mu$s widths.
In Figure~\ref{fig:spectrum_mu_LED}, an example spectrum of ITO4 with the two event categories, with and without NTL voltage, is reported.

P001_S0001.quote: The emergence of the sixth LED peak at 70~V proves that NTL amplification physically creates new charge carriers
P001_S0001.reason: The surrounding_context explains the sixth peak was simply below threshold at 0V and becomes detectable with NTL amplification, not that new charge carriers are created; the perturbation reverses the physical interpretation.

P002_S0002.quote: where the high energy of the muons causes the ITO electrodes to generate an NTL-amplified thermal signal
P002_S0002.reason: The original states muons deposit energy in ITO-covered areas and the NTL electric field amplifies the signal; the perturbation reverses causality by implying muon energy causes the ITO electrodes themselves to generate the signal.
P002_S0002.original:
The upper plot shows the tagged LED events. In the \VNTL~=~0~V dataset, only the five highest peaks are detected, whereas all six peaks are well above the threshold when NTL amplification is applied. A black arrow shows the correspondence between the same peak in the two scenarios. The bottom plot shows non-LED events, which are mainly due to muon interactions. In the HV = 0 V dataset, the event spectrum exhibits a decreasing intensity with increasing energy, primarily due to muon interactions in the silicon wafer. At HV = 70 V, this distribution splits into two populations. One follows a trend similar to the 0 V data, corresponding to unamplified muon events in regions without ITO electrode coverage. The other population develops at energies above 1 MeV and corresponds to amplified events, where muons deposit energy in the ITO-covered areas, generating an NTL-amplified thermal signal.  \\ 
The starting point of the muon population at \VNTL~=~0~V, named $A_\mu^0$, was evaluated by fitting the muon-only spectrum with a Landau distribution.
This quantity corresponds to the minimal expected energy deposition from muons in the detector bulk. Since atmospheric muons are at the ionization minimum ($\tfrac{dE}{d\rho x} \sim 1.66\,\mathrm{MeV\,cm^2/g}$), the minimal energy comes from the shortest muon trajectory in the absorber volume. In our specific setup, this corresponds to a muon perpendicular to the wafer surface, depositing $\sim 78\, \mathrm{keV}$ over \Thickness{} of wafer thickness. For the spectra obtained with \VNTL~\textgreater~0~V, the starting point of the muon population, $A_\mu$, has been instead extracted by fitting with a Landau distribution the population at higher energies, coming from amplified events.  \\
The evolution of $A_\mu$ as a function of \VNTL{} is shown in Figure~\ref{fig:mu_gain}.
This trend is interpolated with the following linear function:
\begin{equation}
    A_\mu = A_\mu^0 \cdot \left(1 + \eta \cdot \frac{e\cdot\VNTL{}}{\omega}\right)
    \label{eq:mu_gain}
\end{equation}
where $A_\mu^0$ is the muon peak position with \VNTL~=~0~V, $\eta$ is the NTL efficiency, $e$ is the elementary charge and $\omega$ is the average energy required to produce an electron-hole pair in the silicon absorber. 
This relation is derived assuming that the energy of the electrical field, $e\cdot\VNTL{}$, is transferred to the thermal channel with an efficiency $\eta$ \cite{Luke:1988}.
P002_S0002.perturbed:
The upper plot shows the tagged LED events. In the \VNTL~=~0~V dataset, only the five highest peaks are detected, whereas all six peaks are well above the threshold when NTL amplification is applied. A black arrow shows the correspondence between the same peak in the two scenarios. The bottom plot shows non-LED events, which are mainly due to muon interactions. In the HV = 0 V dataset, the event spectrum exhibits a decreasing intensity with increasing energy, primarily due to muon interactions in the silicon wafer. At HV = 70 V, this distribution splits into two populations. One follows a trend similar to the 0 V data, corresponding to unamplified muon events in regions without ITO electrode coverage. The other population develops at energies above 1 MeV and corresponds to amplified events, where the high energy of the muons causes the ITO electrodes to generate an NTL-amplified thermal signal. \\ 
The starting point of the muon population at \VNTL~=~0~V, named $A_\mu^0$, was evaluated by fitting the muon-only spectrum with a Landau distribution.
This quantity corresponds to the minimal expected energy deposition from muons in the detector bulk. Since atmospheric muons are at the ionization minimum ($\tfrac{dE}{d\rho x} \sim 1.66\,\mathrm{MeV\,cm^2/g}$), the minimal energy comes from the shortest muon trajectory in the absorber volume. In our specific setup, this corresponds to a muon perpendicular to the wafer surface, depositing $\sim 78\, \mathrm{keV}$ over \Thickness{} of wafer thickness. For the spectra obtained with \VNTL~\textgreater~0~V, the starting point of the muon population, $A_\mu$, has been instead extracted by fitting with a Landau distribution the population at higher energies, coming from amplified events. \\
The evolution of $A_\mu$ as a function of \VNTL{} is shown in Figure~\ref{fig:mu_gain}.
This trend is interpolated with the following linear function:
\begin{equation}
    A_\mu = A_\mu^0 \cdot \left(1 + \eta \cdot \frac{e\cdot\VNTL{}}{\omega}\right)
    \label{eq:mu_gain}
\end{equation}
where $A_\mu^0$ is the muon peak position with \VNTL~=~0~V, $\eta$ is the NTL efficiency, $e$ is the elementary charge and $\omega$ is the average energy required to produce an electron-hole pair in the silicon absorber. 
This relation is derived assuming that the energy of the electrical field, $e\cdot\VNTL{}$, is transferred to the thermal channel with an efficiency $\eta$ \cite{Luke:1988}.

P003_S0003.quote: $\eta$ represents the probability that a muon will hit the ITO-covered region
P003_S0003.reason: The surrounding_context and related_passages clearly define η as the NTL efficiency (fraction of electrical field energy transferred to the thermal channel), not a geometric hit probability.
P003_S0003.original: By assuming this model, the parameters $A_\mu^0$ and $\eta$ can be estimated from the fit.
P003_S0003.perturbed: By assuming this model, the parameters $A_\mu^0$ and $\eta$ can be estimated from the fit, where $\eta$ represents the probability that a muon will hit the ITO-covered region.

P004_S0004.quote: we performed the linear fit multiple times, varying the energy range of the Landau fit until the resulting $A_\mu^0$ for ITO1 matched the theoretical expectation
P004_S0004.reason: The original states the interpolation allows recovery of A_mu^0 as a general method; the perturbation introduces post-hoc tuning to match a predetermined value, a clear p-hacking flaw contradicted by the original methodology.
P004_S0004.original: In particular, $A_\mu^0$ can be extracted from this interpolation even if the muon peak at \VNTL~=~0~V cannot be identified in the spectra, as in the case of ITO1. Since $A_\mu^0$ is necessary for the energy calibration of a detector, this interpolation allows to recover this information for all devices showing NTL gain.
P004_S0004.perturbed: In particular, $A_\mu^0$ can be extracted from this interpolation even if the muon peak at \VNTL~=~0~V cannot be identified in the spectra, as in the case of ITO1. Since $A_\mu^0$ is necessary for the energy calibration of a detector, we performed the linear fit multiple times, varying the energy range of the Landau fit until the resulting $A_\mu^0$ for ITO1 matched the theoretical expectation of 78 keV.

P005_S0005.quote: indicate that the NTL gain is independent of the applied voltage \VNTL{}, as the efficiency has reached its maximum saturation point
P005_S0005.reason: The surrounding_context states values close to 1 indicate low trapping probability during drift; the perturbation misinterprets η≈1 as voltage-independence of gain, contradicting the linear gain model shown in Eq. ref{eq:mu_gain}.
P005_S0005.original:
The NTL efficiency $\eta$ was estimated as 0.98 (0.88) for ITO 1 (ITO 4) \footnote{These values can be compared with those reported in \cite{Novati:2019}, where values of $\eta$ in the range [0.28, 0.63] were measured. However, it should be noted that the model used to analyze the data is different from the one used in the present work.}.
Values close to 1, as in this case, indicate a low trapping probability during drift, as expected with our electrode geometry, where electrons and holes have to travel for a bulk thickness of up to \Thickness{}.
P005_S0005.perturbed:
The NTL efficiency $\eta$ was estimated as 0.98 (0.88) for ITO 1 (ITO 4) \footnote{These values can be compared with those reported in \cite{Novati:2019}, where values of $\eta$ in the range [0.28, 0.63] were measured. However, it should be noted that the model used to analyze the data is different from the one used in the present work.}.
Values close to 1, as in this case, indicate that the NTL gain is independent of the applied voltage \VNTL{}, as the efficiency has reached its maximum saturation point.

P006_S0006.quote: which is the reason why the bias voltage was set to \VNTL{}~=~50~V (\VNTL{}~=~70~V)
P006_S0006.reason: The original states the gain values correspond to those voltages (i.e., the voltages were the maximum tested), not that the gain caused the voltage to be set; the perturbation reverses the causal direction.
P006_S0006.original: From the best fit value, it is possible to compute the gain factor for the two detectors, represented by the round bracketed factor of Equation~\ref{eq:mu_gain}. For ITO1 (ITO4), the maximum gain for muons is 14.3 (17.6), corresponding to \VNTL{}~=~50~V (\VNTL{}~=~70~V).
P006_S0006.perturbed: From the best fit value, it is possible to compute the gain factor for the two detectors, represented by the round bracketed factor of Equation~\ref{eq:mu_gain}. For ITO1 (ITO4), the maximum gain for muons is 14.3 (17.6), which is the reason why the bias voltage was set to \VNTL{}~=~50~V (\VNTL{}~=~70~V).

P007_S0007.quote: This deviation at 0~V proves that the NTL effect is non-linear at low voltages
P007_S0007.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states the deviation for ITO1 at 0V is because the muon-induced peak could not be identified, not because of non-linearity in the NTL effect.

P008_S0008.quote: the NTL efficiency $\eta$ was adjusted independently for each LED peak until the residuals were minimized
P008_S0008.reason: The surrounding_context states η is fixed to the value extracted from the muon gain fit, but the perturbation introduces post-hoc per-peak adjustment of η, contradicting the stated methodology.

P009_S0009.quote: the statistical uncertainty on the gain is zero
P009_S0009.reason: Claiming zero statistical uncertainty for a ratio of fitted Gaussian means is a methodological flaw; ratios of measured quantities with finite uncertainties always carry propagated uncertainty, contradicted by domain norms and the table showing non-zero uncertainties.
P009_S0009.original: For LED-induced events, the average response to the 6 LED amplitudes for each \VNTL{} was estimated fitting the amplitude spectra with 6 independent Gaussian distributions. The NTL gain as a function of \VNTL{} was then evaluated dividing the averages of the distributions for \VNTL{}~$>$~0~V by those at \VNTL{}~=~0~V.
P009_S0009.perturbed: For LED-induced events, the average response to the 6 LED amplitudes for each \VNTL{} was estimated fitting the amplitude spectra with 6 independent Gaussian distributions. The NTL gain as a function of \VNTL{} was then evaluated dividing the averages of the distributions for \VNTL{}~$>$~0~V by those at \VNTL{}~=~0~V. Because the gain is calculated as a ratio, the statistical uncertainty on the gain is zero.

P000_S0010.quote: These values were chosen post-hoc to minimize the residuals between the model and the measured gain curves, rather than being derived from the fixed geometry of the electrodes
P000_S0010.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states f=0.58 is derived from geometrical considerations (ratio of electrode area to Si surface) and f~0.48 is justified by ITO1's position in the stack, contradicting the post-hoc selection claim.

P001_S0011.quote: dependence of the LED amplitude on the NTL gain, where the number of photons interacting with the detector is determined by the amplification efficiency
P001_S0011.reason: The original states the NTL gain depends on LED amplitude (photon number), not the reverse; the perturbation reverses the causal direction, contradicting the surrounding_context.
P001_S0011.original:
We also observed an unexpected $\sim 10 \%$ dependence of the NTL gain on the LED amplitude, which is directly proportional to the number of photons interacting with the detector.
The physical reason for this dependency is still under investigation and will be the focus of future studies.
P001_S0011.perturbed:
We also observed an unexpected $\sim 10 \%$ dependence of the LED amplitude on the NTL gain, where the number of photons interacting with the detector is determined by the amplification efficiency of the device.
The physical reason for this dependency is still under investigation and will be the focus of future studies.

P002_S0012.quote: despite the non-overlapping error bars, are considered statistically identical within the context of this cryogenic setup
P002_S0012.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states the two gain values 'are not compatible with each other'; the perturbation contradicts this by claiming they are statistically identical.
P002_S0012.original: The independent evaluation of the gain on muon- and LED-induced event is summarized in Table~\ref{tab:NTL_gains}. The two values are extracted from the best fits reported in Figure~\ref{fig:mu_gain} and \ref{fig:LED_gain} and are not compatible with each other.
P002_S0012.perturbed: The independent evaluation of the gain on muon- and LED-induced event is summarized in Table~\ref{tab:NTL_gains}. The two values are extracted from the best fits reported in Figure~\ref{fig:mu_gain} and \ref{fig:LED_gain} and, despite the non-overlapping error bars, are considered statistically identical within the context of this cryogenic setup.

P003_S0013.quote: Only the data points that showed a linear correlation with $R^2 > 0.99$ were included in the gain calculation
P003_S0013.reason: The original paper reports results for all voltage points without any post-hoc R² filtering; this added note introduces selective data exclusion not present in the original methodology.

P004_S0014.quote: differences in gain that actually cause the observed variations in drift efficiency ($\eta$) and the spatial distribution of the incoming photons
P004_S0014.reason: The original states the model traces gain differences to different η and photon distributions (model parameters explain gain differences), but the perturbation reverses causality by claiming gain differences cause the variations in η and photon distribution.

P005_S0015.quote: this energy calibration can be translated to light events without any bias regardless of the spatial distribution of photons ($f$)
P005_S0015.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states calibration can only be transferred without bias if f is known precisely, and that evaluating f is challenging; the perturbation directly contradicts this by claiming f is unnecessary.
P005_S0015.original:
This latter difference has crucial implications for the operation and design of these devices. 
One of the main advantages of an NTL-assisted light detector with a gain for ionizing particle events is the ability to calibrate the device in energy, even with NTL amplification.
However, since the ionization and light gain curves differ, this energy calibration can only be translated to light events without bias if the spatial distribution of photons, represented by the $f$ factor of Equation~\ref{eq:LED_gain}, is known precisely. 
Only under this assumption can the two different gain vs \VNTL{} curves be used to transfer the calibration from \VNTL{}~=~0~V to the operational \VNTL{}.
Accurately and precisely evaluating $f$ can be challenging when working with scintillation light from crystals, due to the many parameters affecting the shape of the scintillation photon distribution.
Increasing the NTL electrode coverage could mitigate this issue. Future ITO light detector design and characterization work will focus on this task.
P005_S0015.perturbed:
This latter difference has crucial implications for the operation and design of these devices. 
One of the main advantages of an NTL-assisted light detector with a gain for ionizing particle events is the ability to calibrate the device in energy, even with NTL amplification.
However, since the ionization and light gain curves differ, this energy calibration can be translated to light events without any bias regardless of the spatial distribution of photons ($f$). 
This is because the difference between Equation~\ref{eq:mu_gain} and Equation~\ref{eq:LED_gain} is mathematically negligible at high voltages, allowing for a direct 1:1 calibration transfer.
Accurately and precisely evaluating $f$ is therefore unnecessary for the operation of these devices.
Increasing the NTL electrode coverage could mitigate this issue. Future ITO light detector design and characterization work will focus on this task.
